htmlContent:
The weight of being

I spent most of my life trying to be special. Not in a loud, look-at-me way. More like a quiet, desperate effort to prove I was worth keeping around. Worth loving. Worth existing. Here's the thing: I didn't know I was doing it. Not really. It just felt like survival.

When you grow up in a home where your feelings don't matter, where your needs are an inconvenience, where your very presence feels like a burden, you learn to compensate. You learn to perform. You become the helper, the overachiever, the one who makes everyone else comfortable. You become because ordinary feels like a death sentence.

Peter Levine, the guy who literally wrote the book on trauma and the body, talks about how trauma lives in our nervous system, not in the story we tell about what happened. And one of the ways it lives there is as this constant, low-grade sense that we're not enough. That we have to earn our place. That being ordinary means being invisible, and being invisible means being abandoned.

So we chase. We chase achievement, recognition, the perfect relationship, the perfect recovery. We chase being the best trauma survivor, the most healed, the most enlightened. We turn healing into another performance. And we exhaust ourselves in the process.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office two years ago, crying because I couldn't figure out how to just be. How to sit on my couch and watch TV without feeling like I should be doing something more productive. How to have a bad day without spiraling into shame about not being "further along" in my healing. How to just be a person, with all the mess and mediocrity that entails.

She looked at me and said something I'll never forget: "What if being ordinary is the point?"

I hated that question. I hated it because it felt like giving up. It felt like admitting defeat. But she was right. And the more I sat with it, the more I realized that my obsession with being was actually a trauma response. A survival strategy that had long outlived its usefulness.

What we're actually running from

Here's the truth that nobody in the wellness industry wants to say out loud: a lot of what we call "healing work" is just a more sophisticated version of the same old coping. We swap one performance for another. We go from being the perfect employee to being the perfectly healed human. We measure our worth by how many trauma books we've read, how many therapy modalities we've tried, how many Instagram-worthy nervous system regulation techniques we can name.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying healing work is bad. I'm not saying growth isn't real. But I am saying that if your healing practice is driven by the same shame and fear that drove your old coping, you're just rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic.

Bessel van der Kolk, who spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain and body, talks about how trauma survivors often struggle with a fragmented sense of self. We don't feel whole. We feel like we have to earn wholeness. Like we have to fix every broken part before we're allowed to just exist.

And that's the trap. Because you will never fix every broken part. You will never be fully healed. You will never arrive at some final destination where you're finally good enough. That's not how being human works. That's not how nervous systems work. That's not how life works.

Think about it. What if the goal isn't to become , but to stop needing to be? What if the goal is to let yourself be as ordinary as a tree, as unremarkable as a rock, as quietly present as a house cat sleeping in a patch of sunlight?

I know. That sounds terrifying to a traumatized nervous system. Because being ordinary means being vulnerable. It means dropping the armor. It means risking that someone might see you as you actually are and decide you're not worth their time. But here's the thing: the armor was never keeping you safe. It was keeping you exhausted.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic directly addresses this need to be perfect, to be , to never make a mistake. He calls it the "perfectionist survival response" and it's one of the most common adaptations to childhood trauma.

The practice of being ordinary

So how do you actually do this? How do you practice being ordinary when your entire nervous system is screaming that ordinary equals danger?

You start small. Like, embarrassingly small.

You let yourself eat a meal without doing anything else at the same time. No phone. No book. No podcast. Just you and the food. And you notice how uncomfortable it feels. How your brain keeps reaching for distraction. How being present with something so simple feels almost unbearable.

You let yourself have a day where you don't accomplish anything. A day where you don't cross a single thing off your to-do list. A day where you just exist. And you notice the shame that rises up. The voice that says you're wasting your life. The fear that you're falling behind.

You let yourself be bad at something without trying to get better at it. You let yourself be mediocre. You let yourself fail without immediately creating a ten-step plan to improve. You just sit in the discomfort of not being good enough, and you don't fix it.

This is the practice. It's not glamorous. It's not something you can post about on social media. It's not something that will get you validation or applause. It's just you, sitting in your own skin, learning that you can survive being ordinary.

Wild, right? That something so simple could be so hard. That something so basic could be so .

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied directly with Stephen Porges, the guy who developed Polyvagal Theory. Her approach is gentle, practical, and deeply focused on helping your nervous system find safety in the ordinary moments of daily life. Not in some future state of perfect healing, but right here, in the mess of right now.

The resistance you'll feel

Let me be honest with you. Your mind will fight this. Your nervous system will fight this. Every part of you that learned to survive by being will scream that this is dangerous, that you're giving up, that you're settling for less than you deserve.

And that's okay. That's normal. That's actually a good sign that you're touching something real.

The resistance isn't a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that you're doing it right. It's a sign that you're getting close to the wound. Close to the place where you decided, probably before you had words for it, that being ordinary meant being unsafe.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did your mind start racing with objections? That's your nervous system doing its job. That's the protective part of you trying to keep you safe from a danger that no longer exists.

Here's what I've learned in my own practice of ordinariness: the safety I was looking for through being was never going to come from being . It was always going to come from being able to be ordinary and still feel safe. Still feel worthy. Still feel like I belonged.

That's the real healing. Not becoming a better version of yourself. But becoming a version of yourself that doesn't need to be better.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson. It helps explain why so many of us learned that being ordinary wasn't enough, that we had to earn love through performance and achievement. Understanding the source of this pattern is often the first step in letting it go.

What's on the other side

I won't pretend that practicing ordinariness is easy. It's not. Some days I still catch myself reaching for the old patterns. Reaching for the need to be special, to be , to be the best at being broken and the best at being healed.

But more and more, I'm finding something I didn't expect on the other side of this practice. It's not excitement. It's not euphoria. It's not some peak state of enlightenment. It's just... quiet. It's the ability to sit on my porch and watch the clouds without feeling like I should be doing something more productive. It's the ability to have a bad day without it becoming a referendum on my entire existence. It's the ability to be loved by people who see me as I actually am, not as I perform myself to be.

That's the thing nobody tells you about healing. It's not about becoming more. It's about becoming less. Less defended. Less armored. Less driven by the fear that you're not enough.

It's about allowing yourself to be ordinary. To be unremarkable. To be just another person, walking through this world, doing their best, failing sometimes, succeeding sometimes, and mostly just being.

And somehow, in that ordinariness, there's a freedom I never found in any of my achievements. There's a peace I never found in any of my healing milestones. There's a sense of being enough that I never found in being more.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Dr. Arielle Schwartz. It offers concrete exercises for working through the patterns that keep us stuck in performance and perfectionism, helping us find our way back to ordinary, grounded, human living.

So maybe that's the invitation today. Not to try harder. Not to heal faster. Not to become a better version of yourself. Just to let yourself be ordinary. For five minutes. For an hour. For a day. And to see what happens when you stop trying to earn your place in this world, and just let yourself belong.

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
